intro: Rust's ownership model and borrow checker represent a fundamentally different memory management philosophy from Python's garbage collector. When porting Python to Rust, every variable must have a defined lifetime, `list` becomes `Vec<T>`, `dict` becomes `HashMap<K, V>`, and Python exceptions are replaced with Rust's `Result<T, E>` type. The gain is predictable, zero-overhead performance with memory safety guarantees and no GC pauses.

diffs.from: Garbage collected via reference counting (CPython). No manual memory.
diffs.to: Ownership + borrow checker. Compile-time memory safety. No GC pauses.

diffs.from: Exceptions with try/except. Arbitrary nesting allowed.
diffs.to: Result<T, E> and Option<T>. Errors are values, not control flow.

diffs.from: Dynamic. Type hints optional and unenforced at runtime.
diffs.to: Static, expressive, with algebraic data types (enum with data), traits, and lifetimes.

diffs.from: list (dynamic), dict (hash map), set — all built-in and zero friction.
diffs.to: Vec<T>, HashMap<K,V>, HashSet<T> from std::collections. Must import explicitly.

diffs.from: Interpreted; CPython has GIL. Typically 10–100x slower than Rust.
diffs.to: Compiled to native code. Zero-cost abstractions. Comparable to C/C++ throughput.

examples.title: Function with fallible return
examples.description: Python exceptions map to Rust's Result<T, E>. The ? operator propagates errors ergonomically.

examples.title: Struct and implementation block
examples.description: Python classes map to Rust struct + impl blocks. The derive macro generates boilerplate.

tips.title: Wrap return values in Result/Option
tips.body: Every function that can fail returns Result<T, E>. Functions that may return nothing return Option<T>. Use the ? operator to propagate errors up the call stack.

tips.title: list → Vec<T>
tips.body: Python list becomes Vec<T>. Literal [1,2,3] becomes vec![1,2,3]. list.append(x) becomes vec.push(x). list comprehensions become .iter().filter().map().collect().

tips.title: dict → HashMap<K,V>
tips.body: Python dict becomes std::collections::HashMap<K,V>. Must be imported. Initialize with HashMap::new() or the hashmap! macro from external crates.

tips.title: String vs &str
tips.body: Python str maps to both: String (owned, heap-allocated) and &str (borrowed string slice). Function parameters usually accept &str; owned storage uses String.
